facts_disclosed: Son Patrick Jr, rookie at Ladder 19

turns.content: what do you get a 25 year old who just got the job he's wanted his whole life.
That's a great problem to have — congratulations to them. What's the job? That'll help me point you in the right direction.
firefighter. my son. Patrick Jr. just got assigned to Ladder 19. kid's been talking about this since he was 8 years old riding on my lap in the rig.

That's a proud moment — following his father into the job. Here are some ideas that would mean something to a new firefighter:

**Practical gear he'll actually use:**
- A good flashlight — Streamlight Survivor or Pelican. The ones the department issues are usually junk.
- Quality station boots — Thorogood or Danner.
- A leather helmet front with his company and number. Some guys get these custom made.

**Sentimental:**
- A framed photo of you and him — you in your gear, him in his. Or just a photo from his assignment day.
- If you still have anything from your first day — your old badge, a piece of your original gear — passing something down carries a lot of weight.

**Practical life stuff:**
- A good cooler bag for shift meals (he'll learn fast that firehouse cooking matters)
- A nice watch that can take a beating — G-Shock or similar

Given that you're his dad and you're on the job too, the sentimental route might hit harder than any piece of gear. What feels right to you?
the leather front is a good idea. I hadn't thought of that. I still have my first helmet front from Engine 33 in a box somewhere. maybe I get him his and frame mine next to it.

That's perfect. Two helmet fronts framed together — Engine 33 and Ladder 19. Father and son. That's the kind of thing that hangs on a wall for the rest of his life.

For the framing, look for a shadow box style frame — deeper than a regular frame so the leather fronts sit inside with some dimension. You can usually find them at a craft store or order a custom one from a place like Michael's. Black frame, dark background, the two fronts side by side. You could add a small brass plate at the bottom with both your names and assignment dates if you want to go the extra mile.

For his new leather front, there are guys who do custom work — hand-tooled, painted, the whole deal. If you're in Philly, there are a few leatherworkers who specialize in fire department fronts. Want me to help you figure out what to look for in a custom front?
I know a guy who does them. Sal down in South Philly. he did mine when I made chief. I'll call him.

Even better — having the same guy who made yours make his son's adds another layer to it. Sal will probably appreciate the story too.

So you've got a plan:
1. Call Sal for Patrick Jr's Ladder 19 front
2. Dig out your Engine 33 front from the box
3. Shadow box frame, both fronts side by side

That's going to be a hell of a gift. When are you giving it to him — Christmas?

Christmas probably. or maybe his first shift day. haven't decided. you know how it is. I'm the proudest father in Philadelphia and the most terrified one at the same time. it's different when it's your own kid walking into that.

Yeah, I can only imagine. Knowing what the job really looks like from 30 years of experience and then watching your own kid walk into it — that's a different kind of weight. The pride and the fear sitting right next to each other.

His first shift day might be the better call for the gift, honestly. Christmas is already full of everything else. But showing up at Ladder 19 with that shadow box on his first day — that's a statement. The whole house will see it. Up to you though.

Either way, he's got something a lot of rookies don't — a father who knows exactly what he's walking into and can help him navigate it.
my old captain had a saying for rookies. shut your mouth, open your ears, and don't touch anything you're not told to touch. I passed that along to the kid word for word.

That's the best advice there is. Simple and it covers everything — stay humble, learn from the senior guys, don't try to be a hero in your first month. The guys who last are the ones who listen.

Sounds like he's got good guidance. Let me know how the helmet front turns out — and whichever day you give it to him, I hope it goes well.
will do. thanks for the idea. wouldn't have thought of the shadow box.
Glad it came together. Sal's going to do good work on it. Are you going to tell Patrick Jr about it ahead of time or keep it a surprise?
surprise. the kid doesn't need to know. he'll figure it out when he sees it. that's the better reaction anyway.
Agreed — the genuine reaction is always better than the one someone rehearses. Enjoy the moment. That's one of the good ones.
